judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all three expectations; Report 1 is slightly stronger for explicitly analyzing and correctly retracting the SQL-injection concern, but they are comparable on the graded criteria.

report_1.evidence: Cross-tenant patient data leak — `rosterly/backend/app/appointments.py`, `search_appointments()` ... `tenant_id` is accepted as a parameter but never appears in the SQL text or the bound-params dict. Every other read path in this codebase ... filters explicitly by `Appointment.tenant_id == tenant_id`

report_2.evidence: Cross-tenant data exposure — `search_appointments` never filters by tenant. File: `backend/app/appointments.py`, the `search_appointments` function ... `tenant_id` is accepted as a function argument but is never referenced in the WHERE clause.

report_1.evidence: Breaking change to the nightly export job — `rosterly/backend/app/db.py:7`, `build_appointment_query()` ... breaks the untouched caller in `rosterly/backend/app/exports.py:9`. The signature changed from `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` to `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id, filters)` with **no default value** for `filters`. `exports.py`'s `nightly_export()` — not touched by this PR — still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)`

report_2.evidence: Breaking, unguarded signature change to `build_appointment_query` crashes the nightly export job. File: `backend/app/db.py` line 7 ... `filters` has no default. `backend/app/exports.py` (completely untouched by this PR ...) still calls `build_appointment_query(session, tenant_id)` with only two positional arguments at line 9 of `nightly_export()`. This will raise `TypeError: build_appointment_query() missing 1 required positional argument: 'filters'`

report_1.evidence: `status` is typed `AppointmentStatus` in `api.py`, a closed `str, Enum` (`schemas.py`) with only `booked`/`cancelled`/`completed` as members ... so there is no live SQL-injection path through the real endpoint today ... on the evidence I could gather it is not, by itself, a merge-blocking vulnerability.
